bibdata.title: Evaluation of measurement data
An introduction to the "Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement" and related documents
Évaluation des données de mesure
Une introduction au "Guide pour l’expression de l’incertitude de mesure" et aux documents qui le concernent

organization.name: Bureau International des Poids et Mesures
Bureau International des Poids et Mesures
Bureau International des Poids et Mesures

editorialgroup.committee: Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology
Comité commun pour les guides en métrologie

clause.p: © JCGM 2009
Copyright of this JCGM guidance document is shared jointly by the JCGM member organizations (BIPM, IEC, IFCC, ILAC, ISO, IUPAC, IUPAP and OIML).

clause.title: Copyright
clause.p: Even if electronic versions are available free of charge on the website of one or more of the JCGM member organizations, economic and moral copyrights related to all JCGM publications are internationally protected. The JCGM does not, without its written authorisation, permit third parties to rewrite or re-brand issues, to sell copies to the public, or to broadcast or use on-line its publications. Equally, the JCGM also objects to distortion, augmentation or mutilation of its publications, including its titles, slogans and logos, and those of its member organizations.

clause.title: Official versions and translations
clause.p: The only official versions of documents are those published by the JCGM, in their original languages.

The JCGM’s publications may be translated into languages other than those in which the documents were originally published by the JCGM. Permission must be obtained from the JCGM before a translation can be made. All translations should respect the original and official format of the formulae and units (without any conversion to other formulae or units), and contain the following statement (to be translated into the chosen language):

quote.p: All JCGM’s products are internationally protected by copyright. This translation of the original JCGM document has been produced with the permission of the JCGM. The JCGM retains full internationally protected copyright on the design and content of this document and on the JCGM’s titles, slogan and logos. The member organizations of the JCGM also retain full internationally protected right on their titles, slogans and logos included in the JCGM’s publications. The only official version is the document published by the JCGM, in the original languages.

clause.p: The JCGM does not accept any liability for the relevance, accuracy, completeness or quality of the information and materials offered in any translation. A copy of the translation shall be provided to the JCGM at the time of publication.

clause.title: Reproduction
clause.p: The JCGM’s publications may be reproduced, provided written permission has been granted by the JCGM. A sample of any reproduced document shall be provided to the JCGM at the time of reproduction and contain the following statement:A

quote.p: This document is reproduced with the permission of the JCGM, which retains full internationally protected copyright on the design and content of this document and on the JCGM’s titles, slogans and logos. The member organizations of the JCGM also retain full internationally protected right on their titles, slogans and logos included in the JCGM’s publications. The only official versions are the original versions of the documents published by the JCGM.

clause.title: Disclaimer
clause.p: The JCGM and its member organizations have published this document to enhance access to information about metrology. They endeavor to update it on a regular basis, but cannot guarantee the accuracy at all times and shall not be responsible for any direct or indirect damage that may result from its use. Any reference to commercial products of any kind (including but not restricted to any software, data or hardware) or links to websites, over which the JCGM and its member organizations have no control and for which they assume no responsibility, does not imply any approval, endorsement or recommendation by the JCGM and its member organizations.

foreword.title: Foreword
foreword.p: In 1997 a Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology (JCGM), chaired by the Director of the BIPM, was created by the seven international organizations that had originally in 1993 prepared the ‘Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement’ (GUM) and the ‘International vocabulary of metrology { basic and general concepts and associated terms’ (VIM). The JCGM assumed responsibility for these two documents from the ISO Technical Advisory Group 4 (TAG4).

The Joint Committee is formed by the BIPM with the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC), the International Federation of Clinical Chemistry and Laboratory Medicine (IFCC), the International Laboratory Accreditation Cooperation (ILAC), the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), the International Union of Pure and Applied Physics (IUPAP), and the International Organization of Legal Metrology (OIML).

JCGM has two Working Groups. Working Group 1, ‘Expression of uncertainty in measurement’, has the task to promote the use of the GUM and to prepare Supplements and other documents for its broad application. Working Group 2, ‘Working Group on International vocabulary of basic and general terms in metrology (VIM)’, has the task to revise and promote the use of the VIM. For further information on the activity of the JCGM, see www.bipm.org.
The present document has been prepared by Working Group 1 of the JCGM, and has bene ted from detailed reviews undertaken by member organizations of the JCGM.
This document constitutes one part in a series of JCGM documents under the generic heading Evaluation of measurement data. The parts in the series are

li.p: JCGM 100:2008. Evaluation of measurement data — Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement (GUM) (see ),
JCGM 101:2008. Evaluation of measurement data — Supplement 1 to the “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” — Propagation of distributions using a Monte Carlo method (see ),
JCGM 102. Evaluation of measurement data — Supplement 2 to the “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” — Models with any number of output quantities,
JCGM 103. Evaluation of measurement data — Supplement 3 to the “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” — Modelling,
JCGM 104. Evaluation of measurement data — An introduction to the “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” and related documents [this document],
JCGM 105. Evaluation of measurement data — Concepts and basic principles,
JCGM 106. Evaluation of measurement data — The role of measurement uncertainty in conformity assessment, and
JCGM 107. Evaluation of measurement data — Applications of the least-squares method.

introduction.title: Introduction
introduction.p: A statement of measurement uncertainty is indispensable in judging the fitness for purpose of a measured quantity value. At the greengrocery store the customer would be content if, when buying a kilogram of fruit, the scales gave a value within, say, 2 grams of the fruit’s actual weight. However, the dimensions of components of the gyroscopes within the inertial navigation systems of commercial aircraft are checked by measurement to parts in a million for correct functioning.

Measurement uncertainty is a general concept associated with any measurement and can be used in professional decision processes as well as judging attributes in many domains, both theoretical and experimental. As the tolerances applied in industrial production become more demanding, the role of measurement uncertainty becomes more important when assessing conformity to these tolerances. Measurement uncertainty plays a central role in quality assessment and quality standards.

Measurement is present in almost every human activity, including but not limited to industrial, commercial, scientific, healthcare, safety and environmental. Measurement helps the decision process in all these activities. Measurement uncertainty enables users of a measured quantity value to make comparisons, in the context of conformity assessment, to obtain the probability of making an incorrect decision based on the measurement, and to manage the consequential risks.

This document serves as an introduction to measurement uncertainty, the GUM and the related documents indicated in the Foreword. A probabilistic basis for uncertainty evaluation is used. gives acronyms and initialisms used in this document.

In future editions of JCGM 200 (VIM) it is intended to make a clear distinction between the use of the term error as a quantity and as a quantity value. The same statement applies to the term indication. In the current document such a distinction is made. JCGM 200:2008 does not distinguish explicitly between these uses.

clause.title: Scope
clause.p: The Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology (JCGM) has prepared this document to promote the sound evaluation of measurement uncertainty through the use of the GUM (see ) and to provide an introduction to the GUM Supplements and other documents JCGM is producing: JCGM 101:2008 (see ) and references , , , , .

As in the GUM, this document is primarily concerned with the expression of uncertainty relating to the measurement of a well-defined quantity — the measurand (VIM) 2.3 — that can be characterized by an essentially unique true value (VIM) 2.11 NOTE 3. The GUM provides a rationale for not using the term ‘true’, but this term will be kept in this document when there is otherwise a possibility for ambiguity or confusion.

The purpose of the GUM Supplements and the other documents produced by the JCGM is to help with the interpretation of the GUM and enhance its application. The GUM Supplements and the other documents are together intended to have a scope that is considerably broader than that of the GUM.

This document introduces measurement uncertainty, the GUM, and the GUM Supplements and other documents that support the GUM. It is directed predominantly at the measurement of quantities that can be characterized by continuous variables such as length, temperature, time, and amount of substance.
This introductory document is aimed at the following, including but not limited to

li.p: scientific activities and disciplines in general,
industrial activities and disciplines in general,
calibration, testing and inspection laboratories in industry, and laboratories such as those concerned with health, safety and environment, and
evaluation and accreditation bodies.

clause.p: It is hoped that it will also be useful to designers, because a product specification that takes better account of inspection requirements (and the associated measurement) can result in less stringent manufacturing requirements. It is also directed at academia, with the hope that more university departments will include modules on measurement uncertainty evaluation in their courses. As a result, a new generation of students would be better armed to understand and provide uncertainty statements associated with measured quantity values, and thus gain an improved appreciation of measurement.

This introductory document, the GUM, the GUM Supplements and the other documents should be used in conjunction with the ‘International Vocabulary of Metrology — Basic and general concepts and associated terms’ and all three parts of ISO 3534 cited in , which define statistical terms (used in statistics and probability, including applied statistics and design of experiments), and express them in a conceptual framework in accordance with normative terminology practice. The last consideration relates to the fact that the theoretical background of evaluation of measurement data and evaluation of the uncertainty of measurement is supported by mathematical statistics and probability.

The purpose of measurement is to provide information about a quantity of interest — a measurandmeasurand (VIM) 2.3. The measurandmeasurand might be the volume of a vessel, the potential difference between the terminals of a battery, or the mass concentration of lead in a flask of water.

No measurement is exact. When a quantity is measured, the outcome depends on the measuring systemmeasuring system (VIM) 3.2, the measurement procedure, the skill of the operator, the environment, and other effects . Even if the quantity were to be measured several times, in the same way and in the same circumstances, a different indication value (VIM) 4.1 (measured quantity valuequantity valuemeasured (VIM) 2.10) would in general be obtained each time, assuming that the measuring systemmeasuring system has sufficient resolution to distinguish between the indication valuesindication values. Such indication valuesindication values are regarded as instances of an indication quantityindication quantity.

The dispersiondispersion of the indication valuesindication values would relate to how well the measurement is made. Their averageaverage would provide an estimateestimate 1.31 of the true quantity valuequantity valuetrue (VIM) 2.11 that generally would be more reliable than an individual indication value. The dispersiondispersion and the number of indication valuesindication values would provide information relating to the averageaverage value as an estimateestimate of the true quantity value. However, this information would not generally be adequate.

The measuring systemmeasuring system may provide indication valuesindication values that are not dispersed about the true quantity value, but about some value offset from it. The difference between the offset value and the true quantity value is sometimes called the systematic error valueerror valuesystematic (VIM) 2.17. Take the domestic bathroom scales. Suppose they are not set to show zero when there is nobody on the scales, but to show some value offset from zero. Then, no matter how many times the person’s mass were re-measured, the effect of this offset would be inherently present in the averageaverage of the indication valuesindication values. In general, a systematic error, regarded as a quantity, is a component of error that remains constant or depends in a specific manner on some other quantity.

There are two types of measurement error quantityerror quantityrandomerror quantitysystematic, systematic and random (VIM) 2.19. A systematic error (an estimateestimate of which is known as a measurement biasmeasurement bias (VIM) 2.18) is associated with the fact that a measured quantity value contains an offset. A random error is associated with the fact that when a measurement is repeated it will generally provide a measured quantity value that is different from the previous value. It is random in that the next measured quantity value cannot be predicted exactly from previous such values. (If a prediction were possible, allowance for the effect could be made!) In general, there can be a number of contributions to each type of error.

A challenge in measurement is how best to express what is learned about the measurandmeasurand. Expression of systematic and random error values relating to the measurement, along with a best estimateestimatebest of the measurandmeasurand, is one approach that was often used prior to the introduction of the GUM. The GUM provided a different way of thinking about measurement, in particular about how to express the perceived quality of the result of a measurement. Rather than express the result of a measurement by providing a best estimateestimatebest of the measurandmeasurand, along with information about systematic and random error valueserror valuerandomerror valuesystematic (in the form of an ‘error analysis’), the GUM approach is to express the result of a measurement as a best estimateestimatebest of the measurandmeasurand, along with an associated measurement uncertaintymeasurement uncertainty.

One of the basic premises of the GUM approach is that it is possible to characterize the quality of a measurement by accounting for both systematic and random errors on a comparable footing, and a method is provided for doing that (see ). This method refines the information previously provided in an ‘error analysis’, and puts it on a probabilistic basis through the concept of measurement uncertaintymeasurement uncertainty.

Another basic premise of the GUM approach is that it is not possible to state how well the essentially unique true valuetrue value of the measurandmeasurand is known, but only how well it is believed to be known. Measurement uncertainty can therefore be described as a measure of how well one believes one knows the essentially unique true valuetrue value of the measurandmeasurand. This uncertainty reflects the incomplete knowledge of the measurandmeasurand. The notion of ‘belief’ is an important one, since it moves metrology into a realm where results of measurement need to be considered and quantified in terms of probabilities that express degrees of beliefdegrees of belief.

The above discussion concerns the direct measurement of a quantity, which incidentally occurs rarely. The bathroom scales may convert a measured extension of a spring into an estimateestimate of the measurandmeasurand, the mass of the person on the scales. The particular relationship between extension and mass is determined by the calibrationcalibration (VIM) 2.39 of the scales.

A relationship such as that in constitutes a rule for converting a quantity value into the corresponding value of the measurandmeasurand. The rule is usually known as a measurement modelmeasurement model (VIM) 2.48 or simply a model. There are many types of measurement in practice and therefore many rules or models. Even for one particular type of measurement there may well be more than one model. A simple model (for example a proportional rule, where the mass is proportional to the extension of the spring) might be sufficient for everyday domestic use. Alternatively, a more sophisticated model of a weighing, involving additional effects such as air buoyancy, is capable of delivering better results for industrial or scientific purposes. In general there are often several different quantities, for example temperature, humidity and displacement, that contribute to the definition of the measurandmeasurand, and that need to be measured.

Correction terms should be included in the model when the conditions of measurement are not exactly as stipulated. These terms correspond to systematic error valueserror valuesystematic (VIM) 2.17. Given an estimateestimate of a correction term, the relevant quantity should be corrected by this estimateestimate (GUM) 3.2.4. There will be an uncertainty associatedcorrectionassociated uncertainty with the estimateestimate, even if the estimateestimate is zero, as is often the case. Instances of systematic errors arise in height measurement, when the alignment of the measuring instrument is not perfectly vertical, and the ambient temperature is different from that prescribed. Neither the alignment of the instrument nor the ambient temperature is specified exactly, but information concerning these effects is available, for example the lack of alignment is at most 0:001 and the ambient temperature at the time of measurement differs from that stipulated by at most
2 ⁢
°C
2 "unitsml(degC)".

A quantity can depend on time, for instance a radionuclide decaying at a particular rate. Such an effect should be incorporated into the model to yield a measurandmeasurand corresponding to a measurement at a given timequantitytime-dependent.

As well as raw data representing measured quantity values, there is another form of data that is frequently needed in a model. Some such data relate to quantities representing physical constants, each of which is known imperfectly. Examples are material constants such as modulus of elasticity and specific heat. There are often other relevant data given in reference books, calibrationcalibration certificates, etc., regarded as estimateestimates of further quantities.

The items required by a model to define a measurandmeasurand are known as input quantities in a measurement modelmeasurement modelquantityinput (VIM) 2.50. The rule or model is often referred to as a functional relationship (GUM) 4.1. The output quantity in a measurement modelmeasurement modelquantityoutput (VIM) 2.51 is the measurandmeasurand.

Formally, the output quantityquantityoutput, denoted by
Y
ii(Y), about which information is required, is often related to inputquantityinput quantities, denoted by
X

, … ,
X
N
ii(X)_1,..., ii(X)_{ii(N)}, about which information is available, by a measurement modelmeasurement model (GUM) 4.1.1 in the form of a measurement functionmeasurement function (VIM) 2.49
A general expression for a measurement modelmeasurement modelmeasurement modelgeneral form 2.48 note 1 is

It is taken that a procedure exists for calculating
Y

The true valuetrue values of the input quantities
X

ii(X)_1,...,ii(X)_{ii(N)} are unknown. In the approach advocated,

These distributions describe the respective probabilities of their true valuetrue values lying in different intervals, and are assigned based on available knowledgeavailable knowledge concerning
X

The following considerations, which largely apply to unrelated (independent) quantities, can be extended to interrelated quantities.

The use of available knowledgeavailable knowledge to establish a probability distributionprobability distribution to characterize each quantity of interest applies to the
X

Y

Y

Y

Prior knowledge about the true valuetrue value of the output quantity
Y
ii(Y) can also be considered. For the domestic bathroom scales, the fact that the person’s mass is positive, and that it is the mass of a person, rather than that of a motor car, that is being measured, both constitute prior knowledge about the possible values of the measurandmeasurand in this example. Such additional information can be used to provide a probability distributionprobability distribution for
Y
ii(Y) that can give a smaller standard deviationstandard deviation for
Y
ii(Y) and hence a smaller standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty associated with the estimateestimate of
Y
ii(Y) , , .

Further to those in , fundamental concepts and principles of probability theory that underlie the approach advocated for the evaluation and expression of measurement uncertainty are provided in JCGM 105:2008 .
Measurement uncertainty is defined (VIM) 2.26 as

quote.p: non-negative parameter characterizing the dispersion of the quantity values being attributed to a measurand, based on the information used.

clause.p: This definition is consistent with the considerations of and to .

Two representations of a probability distribution [3.1], 2.11 for a random variable
X
ii(X) are used in uncertainty evaluation:

li.p:
the distribution functionprobability distributiondistribution function 3.2, 2.7, a function giving, for every value of its argument, the probability that
X
ii(X) be less than or equal to that value, and
the probability density functionprobability distributionprobability density function 3.3, 2.26, the derivative of the distribution function.

clause.p:
Knowledge of each input quantity
X
i
ii(X)_i in a measurement modelmeasurement model is often summarized by the best estimateestimateestimatebest
x i
x_i and the associated standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty
u
(
x i
)
u(x_i) (see ). If, for any
i
i and
j
j,
X
i
ii(X)_i and
X
j
ii(X)_j are related (dependent)quantitydependent, the summarizing information will also include a measure of the strength of this relationship, specified as a covariancecovariance 2.43 or a correlationcorrelation. If
X
i
ii(X)_i and
X
j
ii(X)_j are unrelated (independent), their covariancecovariance is zero.

The evaluation of measurement data, in the context of the measurement modelmeasurement model (1) or (2), is the use of available knowledge concerning the input quantities
X
1
, … ,
X
N
ii(X)_1,..., ii(X)_{ii(N)}, as represented by the probability distributions used to characterize them, to deduce the corresponding distribution that characterizes the output quantity
Y
ii(Y). The evaluation of measurement data might entail determining only a summarizing description of the latter distribution.

Knowledge about an input quantity
X
i
ii(X)_i is inferred from repeated indication valuesindication valuesrepeated (Type A evaluation of uncertainty)evaluation of uncertaintyType A (GUM) 4.2, (VIM) 2.28, or scientific judgementscientific judgement or other information concerning the possible values of the quantity (Type B evaluation of uncertainty)evaluation of uncertaintyType B (GUM) 4.3, (VIM) 2.29.

In Type Aevaluation of uncertaintyType A evaluations of measurement uncertainty (VIM) 2.28, the assumption is often made that the distribution best describing an input quantity
X
ii(X) given repeated indication values of it (obtained independently) is a Gaussian distributionprobability distributionGaussian 2.50.
X
ii(X) then has expectationexpectation equal to the average indication value and standard deviationstandard deviation equal to the standard deviationstandard deviation of the average. When the uncertainty is evaluated from a small numberindication valuessmall number of indication values (regarded as instances of an indication quantity characterized by a Gaussian distribution), the corresponding distribution can be taken as a
t
t-distributionprobability distribution
t
t 2.53. shows a Gaussian distribution and (broken curve) a
t
t-distribution with four degrees of freedom. Other considerations apply when the indication values are not obtained independentlyindication valuesnot obtained independently.

For a Type Bevaluation of uncertaintyType B evaluation of uncertainty (VIM) 2.29, often the only available information is that
X
ii(X) lies in a specified intervalspecified interval [
a ,
b
a," "b]. In such a case, knowledge of the quantity can be characterized by a rectangular probability distributionprobability distributionrectangular (GUM) 4.3.7, 2.60 with limits
a
a and
b
b (). If different information were available, a probability distribution consistent with that information would be used .

Once the input quantities
X
1
, … ,
X
N
ii(X)_1,..., ii(X)_{ii(N)} have been characterized by appropriate probability distributions, and the measurement modelmeasurement model has been developed, the probability distribution for the measurand
Y
ii(Y) is fully specified in terms of this information (also see ). In particular, the expectationexpectation of
Y
ii(Y) is used as the estimateestimate of
Y
ii(Y), and the standard deviationstandard deviation of
Y
ii(Y) as the standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty associated with this estimateestimate.

depicts the additive measurement functionmeasurement functionmeasurement functionadditive

Y
ii(Y) has a symmetric trapezoidal probability distributionprobability distributiontrapezoidal in this case.

A Gaussian distribution (continuous black curve) and a

clause.p:
Often an interval containing
Y
ii(Y) with a specified probability is required. Such an interval, a coverage intervalcoverage interval (VIM) 2.36, can be deduced from the probability distribution for
Y
ii(Y). The specified probability is known as the coverage probabilitycoverage probability (VIM) 2.37.
For a given coverage probabilitycoverage probability, there is more than one coverage intervalcoverage interval,

li.p: the probabilistically symmetric coverage intervalcoverage intervalcoverage intervalprobabilistically symmetric 3.15, for which the probabilities (summing to one minus the coverage probabilitycoverage probability) of a value to the left and the right of the interval are equal, and

the shortest coverage intervalcoverage intervalcoverage intervalshortest 3.16, for which the length is least over all coverage intervalcoverage intervals having the same coverage probabilitycoverage probability.

clause.p: shows a probability distribution (a truncated and scaled Gaussian distribution, indicated by the decreasing curve) with the endpoints of the shortest (continuous blue vertical lines) and those of the probabilistically symmetric (broken red vertical lines) 95 % coverage intervalcoverage intervals for a quantity characterized by this distribution. The distribution is asymmetricprobability distributionasymmetric and the two coverage intervalcoverage intervals are different (most notably their right-hand endpoints). The shortest coverage intervalcoverage interval has its left-hand endpoint at zero, the smallest possible value for the quantity. The probabilistically symmetric coverage intervalcoverage interval in this case is 15 % longer than the shortest coverage intervalcoverage interval.

Sensitivity coefficientssensitivity coefficient
c 1
, … ,
c
N
c_1,..., c_{ii(N)} (GUM) 5.1.3 describe how the estimateestimate

( y )
u(y).

According to Resolution 10 of the 22nd CGPM (2003) “…​the symbol for the decimal marker shall be either the point on the line or the comma on the line…​”. The JCGM has decided to adopt, in its documents in English, the point on the line.

figure.name: Shortest 95 % coverage intervalcoverage interval (endpoints shown by continuous blue vertical lines) and probabilistically symmetric 95 % coverage intervalcoverage interval (broken red) for a quantity characterized by a truncated and scaled Gaussian distribution ('unit' denotes any unit)

clause.p: The main stages of uncertainty evaluation constitute formulationstages of uncertainty evaluationformulation and calculationstages of uncertainty evaluationcalculation, the latter consisting of propagationpropagation and summarizingsummarizing.
The formulation stage (see ) constitutes

li.p:
defining the output quantityquantityoutput
Y
ii(Y) (the measurandmeasurand),

identifying the input quantitiesquantityinput on which
Y
ii(Y) depends,

developing a measurement modelmeasurement model relating
Y
ii(Y) to the input quantities, and

on the basis of available knowledgeavailable knowledge, assigning probability distributions — Gaussianprobability distributionGaussian, rectangular, etc. — to the input quantities (or a joint probability distributionprobability distribution to those input quantities that are not independent).

clause.p:
The calculation stage (see ) consists of propagating the probability distributionprobability distributions for the input quantities through the measurement modelmeasurement model to obtain the probability distributionprobability distribution for the output quantity
Y
ii(Y), and summarizingsummarizing by using this distribution to obtain

li.p:
the expectation of
Y
ii(Y), taken as an estimate y of
Y
ii(Y),

the standard deviation of
Y
ii(Y), taken as the standard uncertainty
u
( y )
u(y) associated with
y
y (GUM) E.3.2, and

a coverage interval containing
Y
ii(Y) with a specified coverage probability.

clause.title: The formulation stage: developing a measurement modelmeasurement modelstages of uncertainty evaluationformulation

clause.p: The formulation stage of uncertainty evaluation involves developing a measurement modelmeasurement model, incorporating corrections and other effects as necessary. In some fields of measurement, this stage can be very difficult. It also involves using available knowledgeavailable knowledge to characterize the input quantitiesprobability distributionassigned to input quantities in the model by probability distributionprobability distributions. JCGM 103 provides guidance on developing and working with a measurement modelmeasurement model. The assignment of probability distributionprobability distributions to the input quantities in a measurement modelmeasurement model is considered in JCGM 101 6 and JCGM 102 .

A measurement modelmeasurement model relating the input quantities to the output quantity is initially developed. There might be more than one output quantity (see ). The model is formed on theoretical or empirical grounds or both, and generally depends on the metrology discipline, electrical, dimensional, thermal, mass, etc. The model is then augmented by terms constituting further input quantities, describing effects that influence the measurement. JCGM 103 provides guidance on handling these additional effects, which may be categorized into random and systematic effects.
JCGM 103 considers broader classes of measurement modelmeasurement model than does the GUM, categorizing the model according to whether

li.p: the quantities involved are real or complex,
the measurement modelmeasurement model takes the general form (2) or can be expressed as a measurement function (1), and
there is a single output quantity or more than one output quantity (see ).

clause.p: In category , complex quantities occur especially in electrical metrology, and also in acoustical and optical metrology. In category , for a measurement function the output quantity is expressed directly as a formula involving the input quantities, and for a general measurement modelmeasurement model an equation is solved for the output quantity in terms of the input quantities (see ).

Examples from a range of metrology disciplines illustrate various aspects of JCGM 103. Guidance on numerical analysis aspects that arise in treating these examples is given. Guidance also includes the use of changes of variables so that all or some of the resulting quantities are uncorrelated or only weakly correlated.

The GUM and concentrate on measurement modelmeasurement models in the form of measurement functions having a single output quantity
Y
ii(Y). Many measurement problems arise, however, for which there is more than one output quantity, depending on a common set of input quantities. These output quantities are denoted by
Y
1
, … ,
Y
m
ii(Y)_1,..., ii(Y)_m. Instances include an output quantity that is complex, and represented in terms of its real and imaginary components (or magnitude and phase), quantities representing the parameters of a calibration functioncalibration function, and quantities describing the geometry of the surface of an artefact. The GUM does not directly address such models, although examples are given concerning simultaneous resistance and reactance measurement (GUM) H.2 and thermometer calibration (GUM) H.3.

The formulation phase of uncertainty evaluationuncertainty evaluation for the case of more than one measurand is consistent with that for a measurement modelmeasurement model with a single measurand: it comprises developing a model and assigning probability distributionprobability distributions to the input quantitiesquantityinput based on available knowledgeavailable knowledge. As for a measurement modelmeasurement model with a single output quantityquantityoutput, there is an estimateestimate of each input quantity and a standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty associated with that estimateestimate (and possibly covariancescovariance associated with pairs of estimates). Furthermore, since in general each output quantity depends on all the input quantities, in addition to determining estimates of these output quantitiesmeasurement modelwith any number of output quantities and the standard uncertainties associated with these estimates, it is required to evaluate the covariances associated with all pairs of these estimates.
The counterpart of the measurement function (1) for a number m of output quantities is

in which there are
m
m functions
f 1
, … ,
f m
.
f_1,..., f_m. illustrates such a measurement function.

figure.name:
A measurement function with three input quantities
X
1
ii(X)_1,
X
2
ii(X)_2 and
X
3
ii(X)_3, and two output quantities
Y
1
ii(Y)_1 and
Y
2
ii(Y)_2

clause.p: Multistage measurement modelmeasurement models, where the output quantitiesquantityoutput from previous stages become the input quantitiesquantityinput to subsequent stages, are also treated in JCGM 103. A common example of a multistage measurement modelmeasurement modelmeasurement modelmultistage relates to the construction and use of a calibration functioncalibration function (VIM) 2.39 (see ):

li.p: Given quantity valuequantity values provided by measurement standardmeasurement standards, and corresponding indication valuesindication values provided by a measuring system, determine estimates of the parameters of the calibration functioncalibration function. The standard uncertainties associated with the measured quantity valuequantity values and the indication valuesindication values give rise to standard uncertainties associated with these estimates and in general with covariancescovariance associated with all pairs of these estimatesestimate;

Given a further indication value, evaluate the calibration functioncalibration function to provide the corresponding measured quantity valuequantity valuequantity valuemeasured. This step involves the inverse of the calibration functioncalibration functioncalibration functioninverse. The standard uncertainties and covariances associated with the estimates of the parameters of the calibration functioncalibration functioncalibration functionparameter, together with the standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty associated with the further indication value, give rise to a standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty associated with this measured quantity value.

figure.name: A two-stage measurement modelmeasurement model for a calibration functioncalibration function in which quantity values provided by measurement standardmeasurement standards and corresponding indication valuesindication values are used to establish estimates of the parameters of the calibration functioncalibration function, which, given a further indication value, are used to estimate the corresponding measured quantity value

clause.title: The calculation (propagation and summarizing) stage of uncertainty evaluation

clause.p: The propagation stage of uncertainty evaluation is known as the propagation of distributionspropagationof distributions 5.2, various approaches for which are available, including

li.p:
the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework, constituting the application of the law of propagation of uncertaintylaw of propagation of uncertainty, and the characterization of the output quantityquantityoutput
Y
ii(Y) by a Gaussian or a
t
t-distributionprobability distribution
t
tprobability distributionGaussian (see ),

analytic methodsanalytic method, in which mathematical analysis is used to derive an algebraic form for the probability distributionprobability distributionprobability distributiondistribution function for
Y
ii(Y) (see ), and

a Monte Carlo methodMonte Carlo method (MCM), in which an approximation to the distribution function for
Y
ii(Y) is established numerically by making random draws from the probability distributionprobability distributions for the input quantitiesquantityinput, and evaluating the modelmeasurement modelevaluation at the resulting values (see ).

clause.p: For any particular uncertainty evaluation problemuncertainty evaluationproblem, approach , or (or some other approach) is used, being generally approximate, exact, and providing a solution with a numerical accuracy that can be controlled.
The application of approaches and to measurement functions with any number of output quantities, and general measurement modelsmeasurement modelgeneral form, is considered in .

clause.title: The GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework

clause.p: The GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework (GUM) 3.4.85.1 (depicted in ) uses

li.p:
the best estimatesestimatebest
x i
x_i of the input quantities
X
i
ii(X)_i,

the standard uncertainties
u
(
x i
)
u(x_i) associated with the
x i
x_i, and

the sensitivity coefficientsensitivity coefficients
c i
c_i (see )

clause.p:
to form an estimateestimate
y
y of the output quantityquantityoutput
Y
ii(Y) and the associated standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty
u
( y )
u(y).

A variant (GUM) 5.2 of applies when the input quantities are mutually dependentquantitydependent (not indicated in ). By regarding the probability distributionprobability distribution for
Y
ii(Y) as Gaussianprobability distributionGaussian, a coverage intervalcoverage interval for
Y
ii(Y) corresponding to a specified coverage probabilitycoverage probability is also determined (GUM) G.2. When the degrees of freedomdegrees of freedom 2.54 relating to any
u
(
x i
)
u(x_i) is finite, an (effective) degrees of freedomdegrees of freedomeffective relating to
u
( y )
u(y) is determined, and the probability distributionprobability distribution for
Y
ii(Y) taken as a
t
t-distributionprobability distributionstem[t].

There are many circumstances where the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework (GUM) 5 can be applied and leads to valid statements of uncertainty. If the measurement function is linearmeasurement functionlinear in the input quantitiesquantityinput and the probability distributionprobability distributions for these quantities are Gaussianprobability distributionGaussian, the GUM uncertainty framework provides exact results 5.7. Even when these conditionsGUM uncertainty frameworkconditions on use do not hold, the approach can often work sufficiently well for practical purposes 5.8.
There are situations where the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework might not be satisfactory, including those where

li.p: the measurement function is non-linearmeasurement functionnon-linear,
the probability distributionprobability distributions for the input quantities are asymmetric,

the uncertainty contributions
|
c 1
| u
(
x 1
)
, … , |
c
N
| u
(
x
N
)
|c_1| u(x_1),..., |c_{ii(N)}| u(x_{ii(N)}) (see ) are not of approximately the same magnitude (GUM) G.2.2, and

the probability distributionprobability distribution for the output quantityquantityoutput is either asymmetricprobability distributionasymmetric, or not a Gaussian or a
t
t-distribution.

clause.p: Sometimes it is hard to establish in advance that the circumstances hold for the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty frameworkconditions on use to apply.

figure.name:
Measurement uncertainty evaluation using the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework, where the top-left part of the figure (bounded by broken lines) relates to obtaining an estimateestimate y of the output quantity
Y
ii(Y) and the associated standard uncertainty
u
( y )
u(y), and the remainder relates to the determination of a coverage intervalcoverage interval for
Y
ii(Y)

clause.p: The use of the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework becomes more difficult when forming partial derivativepartial derivatives (or numerical approximations to them) for a measurement model that is complicatedmeasurement modelcomplicated, as needed by the law of propagation of uncertainty (possibly with higher-order terms)law of propagation of uncertaintyhigher-order terms (GUM) 5. A valid and sometimes more readily applicable treatment is obtained by applying a suitable Monte Carlo implementationMonte Carlo method of the propagation of distributionspropagationof distributions (see ).

Analytic methods by which an algebraic form for the probability distributionprobability distribution for the output quantity can be obtained do not introduce any approximation, but can be applied only in relatively simple cases. A treatment of such methods is available , . Some cases that can be so handled for a general number
N
ii(N) of input quantitiesquantityinput are linear measurement functionsmeasurement functionlinear (expression (3)), where the probability distributionprobability distributions for all input quantities are Gaussianprobability distributionGaussian, or all are rectangular with the same width. An instance with two input quantitiesquantityinput (
N
= 2
ii(N) = 2), for which the probability distributionprobability distributions for the input quantities are rectangularprobability distributionrectangular, and the probability distributionprobability distribution for the output quantityquantityoutput is trapezoidalprobability distributiontrapezoidal is illustrated in .

Cases where there is one input quantity (
N
= 1
ii(N) = 1) can often be treated analytically, using a formula , pages 57-61, to derive algebraically a probability distributionprobability distribution for the output quantity. Such cases arise in the transformation of measurement unitsmeasurement units, transformation, for example from linear to logarithmic units , pages 95-98.

An advantage of an algebraic solution is that it provides insight through displaying the dependence of the probability distributionprobability distribution for the output quantityquantityoutput on parameters of the probability distributionprobability distributions for the input quantities.

provides detailed information on MCM as an implementation of the propagation of distributionspropagationof distributions 5.9. MCM has fewer conditions associated with its use than the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework 5.10. illustrates the procedure. gives examples to compare MCM with the use of the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty frameworkcompared with Monte Carlo methodMonte Carlo methodcomparison with the GUM uncertainty frameworkMonte Carlo methodconditions on useMonte Carlo methodexamples 9.

provides an adaptive MCMMonte Carlo methodadaptive procedure, in which the number of Monte Carlo trialsMonte Carlo methodnumber of trials is determined automatically by utilizing a measure of convergence of the overall process 7.9.

In there is a procedure that uses MCM to decide whether the application of the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty frameworkvalidition using MCM in any particular case is valid 8.

figure.name:
Measurement uncertainty evaluation using a Monte Carlo method, where the part of the figure to the left of the broken line relates to obtaining an estimateestimate y of the output quantity
Y
ii(Y) and the associated standard uncertainty
u
( y )
u(y), and the remainder relates to the determination of a coverage intervalcoverage interval for
Y
ii(Y)

clause.title: Measurement models with any number of output quantitiesquantityoutputmeasurement modelwith any number of output quantities

clause.p: In order to evaluate the uncertainties and covariancecovariances associated with estimateestimates of the output quantitiesquantityoutput for measurement modelsmeasurement model with any number of output quantities, both the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework and MCMMonte Carlo method, as treated in , require extension. The GUM (GUM) F.1.2.3 outlines such an extension of the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework, but considers it further only in examples.

In JCGM 102 it is stated that the law of propagation of uncertaintylaw of propagation of uncertainty, a main constituent of the GUM uncertainty frameworkGUM uncertainty framework, can succinctly be expressed in an equivalent matrix formmatrix form when applied to a measurement modelmeasurement model having a single output quantityquantityoutput. The matrix expression has the advantage of being suitable as a basis for implementation in softwaresoftware implementation, and for extension to more general types of measurement modelmeasurement model.

That extension is given in JCGM 102 for a measurement function having any number of output quantities. The extension to any number of output quantitiesquantityoutput in a general measurement modelmeasurement modelmeasurement modelwith any number of output quantities (see ) is also treated in JCGM 102.

JCGM 102 also applies MCMMonte Carlo method to measurement models with any number of output quantitiesmeasurement modelwith any number of output quantities. A discrete representation of the probability distributionprobability distributionprobability distributiondiscrete representation for the output quantitiesquantityoutput is provided. Expressions are given for the estimateestimates of the output quantities, the standard uncertaintiesstandard uncertainty associated with these estimateestimates, and the covariancecovariances associated with pairs of these estimateestimates in terms of that representation.

In addition to obtaining estimateestimates of the output quantities, together with the associated standard uncertaintiesstandard uncertainty and covariancecovariances, it might be required to obtain a region containing the output quantitiesquantityoutput with a specified (coverage) probabilitycoverage probability. It is natural to consider the extension to regions of the probabilistically symmetric coverage intervalcoverage intervalprobabilistically symmetric and the shortest coverage intervalcoverage interval. However, there is no natural counterpart of a probabilistically symmetric coverage intervalcoverage interval in the form of a coverage regioncoverage region, whereas there is for a shortest coverage intervalcoverage intervalshortest. The determination of a smallest coverage regioncoverage regionsmallest is generally a difficult task.

In some circumstances, it is reasonable to provide an approximate coverage regioncoverage regionapproximate having simple geometric shape. Two particular forms of coverage regioncoverage region are considered in this regard. One form results from characterizing the output quantitiesquantityoutput by a joint Gaussian distributionprobability distributionjoint Gaussian, for example on the basis of the central limit theoremcentral limit theorem (GUM) G.2, in which case the smallest coverage regioncoverage regionsmallest is bounded by a hyper-ellipsoidhyper-ellipsoid. The other form constitutes a hyper-rectangular coverage regioncoverage regionhyper-rectangular. Procedures for obtaining these forms are provided in JCGM 102.

Conformity assessmentconformityassessment is an area of importance in manufacturing quality controlquality control, legal metrologylegal metrology, and in the maintenance of health and safetyhealth and safety. In the industrial inspectionindustrial inspection of manufactured parts, decisions are made concerning the compatibility of the parts with the design specificationdesign specification. Similar issues arise in the context of regulationregulation (relating to emissions, radiation, drugs, doping control, etc.) concerning whether stipulated limitsstipulated limits for true quantity valuesquantity valuetrue have been surpassed. Guidance is provided in JCGM 106 . Also see reference .

Measurement is intrinsic to conformity assessmentconformityassessment in deciding whether the output quantityquantity, or measurandmeasurand, conforms to a specified requirementspecified requirement. For a single quantity, such a requirement typically takes the form of specification limitsspecification limits that define an interval of permissible quantity valuesquantity valueinterval of permissible. In the absence of uncertainty, a measured quantity valuequantity valuemeasured lying within this interval is said to be conformingquantity valuemeasuredconforming, and non-conformingquantity valuemeasurednon-conforming otherwise. The influence of measurement uncertaintymeasurement uncertainty on the inspection process necessitates a balance of risksriskbalance of between producers and consumers.

The possible valuesquantitypossible values of a quantity
Y
ii(Y) of interest are represented by a probability distributionprobability distribution. The probabilityprobability that
Y
ii(Y) conforms to specificationconformityspecification can be calculated, given this probability distributionprobability distribution and the specification limitsspecification limits.

Because of the incomplete knowledgeincomplete knowledge of the quantityquantity
Y
ii(Y) (as encoded in its probability distributionprobability distribution), there is a riskrisk of a mistaken decisionmistaken decision in deciding conformity to specificationconformityspecification. Such mistaken decisions are of two types: a quantity accepted as conforming might actually be non-conforming, and a quantity rejected as non-conforming might actually conform. The related risks correspond, respectively, to consumer’s riskriskconsumer and producer’s riskriskproducer (see JCGM 106).

By defining an acceptance interval of acceptable measured quantity valuesquantity valuemeasured, the risks of a mistaken decisionmistaken decision concerning acceptanceacceptance or rejectionrejection can be balanced so as to minimize the costs associated with these decisions . The problem of calculating the conformity probabilityprobabilityconformity and the probabilities of the two types of mistaken decision, given the probability distributionprobability distribution, the specification limitsspecification limits, and the limits of the acceptance intervalacceptance interval is addressed in JCGM 106. The choice of acceptance interval limits is a matter that depends on the implications of these mistaken decisions.

Although the probability distributionprobability distribution in to is general, the treatment is then specialized in JCGM 106 to the most important case in practice, namely, when the probability distributionprobability distribution is Gaussianprobability distributionGaussian.

Guidance on the application of the least-squaresleast squares method (also known as least-squares adjustment)least squaresadjustment to data evaluationdata evaluation problems in metrology is provided in JCGM 107 . In such problems there is often an underlying theoretical relationshipunderlying relationship between an independent variablevariableindependent and a dependent variablevariabledependent. This relationship constitutes the basis of a measurement model,parameter adjustmentmeasurement model,parameter adjustment or curve-fittingcurve fitting problem. The input quantitiesquantityinput in the related measurement model are the quantities of which the measured valuesquantity valuemeasuredvariabledependentmeasured valuesvariableindependentmeasured values of the independent and dependent variables are outcomes. The output quantitiesquantityoutput are the quantities representing the required parameters. The manner in which the output quantities are obtained from the input quantities by means of a least-squares procedure defines the measurement model.

In calibrationcalibration terminology (see ), a measured quantity valuequantity valuemeasured of an independent variablevariableindependent would typically be that of a measurement standard. The value of the dependent variablevariabledependent would be an indication valueindication values returned by the measuring systemmeasuring system for the corresponding value of the independent variable. In the curve-fittingcurve fitting context, which includes calibrationcalibration as a special case, the adjustment procedure used in JCGM 107 is a generalized version of the usual least-squares procedureleast squares.

The task is to estimate the parameters (and sometimes even their number) from pairs of measured quantity valuesquantity valuemeasured and the corresponding indication valuesindication values. These pairs, together with the associated standard uncertaintiesstandard uncertainty and, when appropriate, covariancecovariances, constitute the input data to the adjustmentmeasurement modelparameter adjustment.

Typical measurement problems to which JCGM 107 can be applied include (a) linear or non-linear curve-fittingcurve fitting problems, including the case of imperfectly known values of the independent variablevariableindependent, and (b) fitting of general models to estimate parameters in a physical process. The application of JCGM 107 is not restricted to curve-fittingcurve fitting problems in the strictest sense. It can also be used to treat, for instance, convolutionconvolution problems , the adjustment of fundamental constantfundamental constants , and key comparisonkey comparison data evaluationdata evaluation .

For problems of type (a) in , once the least-squaresleast squares method has been used to estimate the parameters of a calibration functionparametercalibration function and evaluate the associated standard uncertaintiesstandard uncertainty and covariancecovariances, the measuring systemmeasuring system will subsequently be used for measurement. The estimates of the parameters of the calibrationcalibration function, together with a particular indication valueindication values, are then used to estimate the corresponding quantity. The standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty associated with this estimate is evaluated using the standard uncertainties and covariancecovariances associated with the parameter estimatesparameterestimation and the standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty associated with the indication value.

It is emphasized in JCGM 107 that the uncertainty structure should be taken fully into account when formulating and solving the least-squaresleast squares problem. ‘Uncertainty structure’ refers to the standard uncertaintiesstandard uncertainty associated with the measured quantity values and indication valuesindication values and any covariancecovariances associated with pairs of these values.measurement uncertainty

For problems of type (b) in , or in terms of determining the parameterscalibration functionparameter in problems of type (a), the adjustment problem is rarely a problem in only one output quantityquantityoutput. Rather, the problem involves a number of output quantities in which the mathematical formulation can conveniently be expressed in terms of matrices. JCGM 107 makes extensive use of matrix formalismmatrix form, which is well adapted to numerical solutionnumerical solution using a computer, as usually required in practice (also see ).

annex.title: Acronyms and initialisms
annex.p: gives acronyms and initialisms used in this document

tr.td: Bureau International des Poids et Mesures
Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement
International Federation of Clinical Chemistry and Laboratory Medicine
International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry
International Union of Pure and Applied Physics
Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology
International Vocabulary of Metrology|Basic and general concepts and associated terms

references.title: Normative references
references.p: The following referenced documents are indispensable for the application of this document. For dated references, only the edition cited applies. For undated references, the latest edition of the referenced document (including any amendments) applies.

bibitem.title: Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement

organization.name: International Bureau of Weights and Measures
International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry
International Union of Pure and Applied Physics
International Federation of Clinical Chemistry and Laboratory Medicine

bibitem.title: Supplement 1 to the “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” — Propagation of distributions using a Monte Carlo method

organization.name: International Bureau of Weights and Measures
International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry
International Union of Pure and Applied Physics
International Federation of Clinical Chemistry and Laboratory Medicine

bibitem.title: International vocabulary of metrology — Basic and general concepts and associated terms (VIM)

organization.name: International Bureau of Weights and Measures
International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry
International Union of Pure and Applied Physics
International Federation of Clinical Chemistry and Laboratory Medicine

bibitem.title: Statistics
Vocabulary and symbols
Part 1: General statistical terms and terms used in probability
Statistics — Vocabulary and symbols — Part 1: General statistical terms and terms used in probability
bibitem.abstract: ISO 3534-1:2006 defines general statistical terms and terms used in probability which may be used in the drafting of other International Standards. In addition, it defines symbols for a limited number of these terms.

bibitem.title: Statistics
Vocabulary and symbols
Part 2: Applied statistics
Statistics — Vocabulary and symbols — Part 2: Applied statistics
bibitem.abstract: ISO 3534-2:2006 defines applied statistics terms, and expresses them in a conceptual framework in accordance with ISO normative terminology practice. Term entries are arranged thematically. An alphabetical index is provided. Standardized symbols and abbreviations are defined. The two principal purposes of ISO 3534-2:2006 are, specifically, to establish a common vocabulary for use throughout ISO/TC 69 standards, together with the broader intent to enhance the preciseness, clarity and cohesiveness in the usage/application of applied statistics generally. The mathematical level has deliberately been kept to a low level in order for the content to be made readily comprehensible to the widest possible readership.

bibitem.title: Statistics
Vocabulary and symbols
Part 3: Design of experiments
Statistics — Vocabulary and symbols — Part 3: Design of experiments

bibitem.formattedref: Bell, S. Measurement Good Practice Guide No. 11. A Beginner’s Guide to Uncertainty of Measurement. Tech. rep., National Physical Laboratory, 1999.
Bernardo, J., and Smith, A. Bayesian Theory. John Wiley & Sons, New York, USA, 2000.
BIPM, IEC, IFCC, ILAC, ISO, IUPAC, IUPAP, and OIML. Evaluation of measurement data — Applications of the least-squares method. Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology, JCGM 107, in preparation. ,
BIPM, IEC, IFCC, ILAC, ISO, IUPAC, IUPAP, and OIML. Evaluation of measurement data — Concepts and basic principles. Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology, JCGM 105, in preparation. ,

BIPM, IEC, IFCC, ILAC, ISO, IUPAC, IUPAP, and OIML. Evaluation of measurement data — Supplement 2 to the “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” — Models with any number of output quantities. Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology, JCGM 102, in preparation. , ,

BIPM, IEC, IFCC, ILAC, ISO, IUPAC, IUPAP, and OIML. Evaluation of measurement data — Supplement 3 to the “Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement” — Modelling. Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology, JCGM 103, in preparation. , ,

BIPM, IEC, IFCC, ILAC, ISO, IUPAC, IUPAP, and OIML. Evaluation of measurement data — The role of measurement uncertainty in conformity assessment. Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology, JCGM 106, in preparation. ,
Casella, G. C., and Berger, R. L. Statistical Inference. Duxbury Press, Pacific Grove, 2001. Second Edition.
Cox, M. G. The evaluation of key comparison data. Metrologia 39 (2002), 589-595.
Cox, M. G., and Harris, P. M. SSfM Best Practice Guide No. 6, Uncertainty evaluation. Tech. Rep. DEM-ES-011, National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, UK, 2006. ,
Cox, M. G., and Harris, P. M. Software specifications for uncertainty evaluation. Tech. Rep. DEM-ES-010, National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, UK, 2006.
Dietrich, C. F. Uncertainty, Calibration and Probability. Adam Hilger, Bristol, UK, 1991.
Elster, C. Calculation of uncertainty in the presence of prior knowledge. Metrologia 44 (2007), 111-116.
EURACHEM/CITAC. Quantifying uncertainty in analytical measurement. Tech. Rep. Guide CG4, EU-RACHEM/CITEC,[EURACHEM/CITAC Guide], 2000. Second edition.
Feller, W. An Introduction to Probability Theory and its Applications, Volume I. Wiley, 1968.
Feller, W. An Introduction to Probability Theory and its Applications, Volume II. Wiley, 1971.
Hibbert, D. B. Quality Assurance for the Analytical Chemistry Laboratory. Oxford University Press, Oxford, UK, 2007.

IEC. IEC Guide 115. Application of uncertainty of measurement to conformity assessment activities in the elec-trotechnical sector. International Electrotechnical Commission, Geneva, Switzerland, 2007.

bibitem.title: Statistical methods
Guidelines for the evaluation of conformity with specified requirements
Part 1: General principles
Statistical methods — Guidelines for the evaluation of conformity with specified requirements — Part 1: General principles
Statistical methods
Guidelines for the evaluation of conformity with specified requirements
Part 1: General principles
Statistical methods — Guidelines for the evaluation of conformity with specified requirements — Part 1: General principles
bibitem.abstract: ISO 10576-1:2002 sets out guidelines for drafting requirements that may be formulated as limiting values for a quantifiable characteristic and for checking conformity to such requirements when the test or measurement result is subject to uncertainty. It is applicable whenever the uncertainty may be quantified according to the principles laid down in the Guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement. The term uncertainty is thus a descriptor for all elements of variation in the measurement result, including uncertainty due to sampling. It is outside the scope of ISO 10576-1:2002 to give rules for how to act when an inconclusive result of a conformity test has been obtained.

bibitem.title: General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories
General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories
General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories
General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories

bibitem.formattedref: Korczynski, M. J., Cox, M. G., and Harris, P. M. Convolution and uncertainty evaluation. In Advanced Mathematical Tools in Metrology VII (Singapore, 2006), P. Ciarlini, E. Felipe, A. B. Forbes, and F. Pavese, Eds., World Scientific, pp. 188-195.
Mohr, P. J., and Taylor, B. N. CODATA recommended values of the fundamental physical constants: 2002. Rev. Mod. Phys. 76 (2004), 4.
NIST. Uncertainty of measurement results.
Possolo, A., and Toman, B. Assessment of measurement uncertainty via observation equations. Metrologia 44 (2007), 464-475.
Rice, J. R. Mathematical Statistics and Data Analysis, second ed. Duxbury Press, Belmont, Ca., USA, 1995.
Weise, K., and Woger, W. A Bayesian theory of measurement uncertainty. Meas. Sci. Technol. 3 (1992), 1-11.
